candidates.text: Nothing like waking up to PagerDuty Alerts because an AWS region has been blown up. I can say this is definitely a first for me

Monitoring alerts fall into two categories: false positives that wake you up at 3am for no reason, and real incidents that somehow never triggered an alert until someone noticed the site was down on Twitter.

Three weeks on-call with alerts at 2 4 6 am and now small mistakes creeping in because sleep is gone.  
No one rotates you out.  
That point where errors start showing up is already too late.  
File the complaint the moment you notice the mistakes. Waiting longer just piles up more risk for you and the system.
I've had my fair share of late nights trying to perfect my app's UI, only to be woken up by an email saying the server went down while I slept, and I'm left scrambling to fix it at 3 am.

It sucks man, putting in so much work and a day before production Go-live, through your own investigation you find out that a team made a mess in production and didn't communicate to the rest of the organisation. Now I have to go find a budget of around R900K to redeploy. FML 😫

You've been on-call for 3 weeks straight.

3 incidents last week alone. 2am. 4am. 6am.

You're making mistakes now. Small ones. But mistakes.

Nobody has offered to rotate you off.

How long before you file a complaint?

Last week, I built a full CI/CD pipeline in Microsoft Azure using Azure CLI + GitHub Actions 

Every git push:
Authenticates via Service Principal
Uploads to Blob $web
Updates live instantly
Broke things, Fixed paths. Debugged auth.
@TechCrushHQ 
#Azure #DevOps #CloudEngineering

The real bottleneck is always the cost vs. risk analysis. Maintaining a warm standby in a second region can double your AWS bill instantly. Most startups pray for the best, but for enterprise apps, if you haven't tested a regional failover, you don't actually have one

Can confirm. We shipped so hard, we broke @coinbase's  internal deployment of GitHub multiple times.

@chintanturakhia is the type of eng leader who refuses to be constrained by what AI tools can do today. He has a vision of where the team needs to be - the shipping velocity, the quality, how eng and design and product should actually work together - and works backwards from first principles to get there.

He's in the weeds more than most ICs, experimenting daily, finding the gaps in what's possible and pushing for solutions until we get there.

I was so lucky to have him as an eng partner in building at @base. Go watch this episode!!
